vignette: A 55-year-old male with cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B, MELD 18) presents with hematemesis. Vitals: BP 85/50, HR 120. He is confused. Exam shows ascites and spider angiomata. What is the FIRST step in management?

options.text: IV octreotide + IV ceftriaxone + IV fluids

explanation.whyCorrect: Variceal hemorrhage: 1) IV fluids, 2) IV octreotide, 3) IV ceftriaxone. Then urgent endoscopy within 12 hours.

whyWrong.D: TIPS is for refractory bleeding.
whyWrong.E: PPIs are for PEPTIC ulcer bleeding.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Variceal bleed: Octreotide + Ceftriaxone + Fluids → Urgent EGD (banding).

vignette: A 42-year-old female presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea, and vomiting after a fatty meal. Lipase is 1,200 U/L. CT shows acute interstitial pancreatitis with no necrosis. What is the MOST important next step?

options.text: Start early enteral nutrition within 24 hours

explanation.whyCorrect: Early enteral nutrition (within 24-48 hours) reduces mortality, infections, and length of stay.

whyWrong.C: NPO and TPN are outdated. Enteral nutrition is superior.
whyWrong.D: ERCP is for gallstone pancreatitis with cholangitis.
whyWrong.E: Surgery is for infected necrosis after 4 weeks.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Pancreatitis: Aggressive IV fluids + Early enteral nutrition = Reduced mortality.

vignette: A 28-year-old female presents with jaundice, fatigue, and arthralgias. Labs: AST 180, ALT 220, ALP 150, Total bilirubin 4.5. ANA positive (1:320), Anti-smooth muscle antibody positive. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: Young female + Hepatitis pattern + ANA + ASMA positive = Autoimmune hepatitis Type 1.

whyWrong.A: PBC has cholestatic pattern + AMA positive.
whyWrong.C: PSC has cholestatic pattern + MRCP shows beaded ducts.
whyWrong.D: Drug-induced would not have positive autoimmune markers.
whyWrong.E: Wilson presents with neuropsychiatric symptoms + KF rings.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Autoimmune hepatitis: Young female + Hepatitis pattern + ANA/ASMA positive. Treatment: Steroids + Azathioprine.

vignette: A 50-year-old male at average risk for colorectal cancer asks about screening options. He prefers a non-invasive test. Which is the MOST appropriate recommendation?

explanation.whyCorrect: CRC screening starts at 45. Non-invasive option: FIT annually. If positive → colonoscopy.

whyWrong.A: Colonoscopy is invasive. Patient requested non-invasive.
whyWrong.C: CT colonography is an option but FIT is preferred.
whyWrong.D: Flex sig is less common now.

explanation.clinicalPearl: CRC screening: Start at 45! Options: Colonoscopy q10yr, FIT q1yr.

vignette: A 48-year-old male with history of heartburn for 10 years presents with progressive dysphagia to solids. EGD shows distal esophageal stricture and biopsy shows intestinal metaplasia without dysplasia. What is the BEST long-term management?

options.text: Endoscopic surveillance every 3-5 years + PPI BID
Endoscopic surveillance every 1 year + PPI daily

explanation.whyCorrect: Barrett esophagus WITHOUT dysplasia: Surveillance every 3-5 years + PPI BID.

whyWrong.B: EMR is for visible lesions or high-grade dysplasia.
whyWrong.D: Annual surveillance is for low-grade dysplasia.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Non-dysplastic Barrett: PPI BID + surveillance q3-5yr.

vignette: A 45-year-old obese female presents with episodic RUQ pain after fatty meals for 3 months. US shows multiple gallstones, CBD 4mm, no wall thickening. LFTs are normal. What is the BEST management?

explanation.whyCorrect: Symptomatic cholelithiasis: Elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy is definitive treatment.

whyWrong.B: ERCP is for choledocholithiasis or cholangitis.
whyWrong.D: Symptomatic gallstones should be treated surgically.
whyWrong.E: Emergency surgery is for acute cholecystitis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Symptomatic cholelithiasis: Elective cholecystectomy. Acute cholecystitis: Early cholecystectomy (within 72h).

vignette: A 28-year-old male with ulcerative colitis (pancolitis) for 5 years presents with worsening bloody diarrhea, urgency, and weight loss. He has failed mesalamine and azathioprine. Colonoscopy shows severe active colitis. What is the BEST next step?

explanation.whyCorrect: Severe UC flare failing mesalamine + thiopurine: Start steroids (prednisone or IV methylprednisolone) for rapid symptom control + Anti-TNF (infliximab) for long-term remission. Anti-TNFs work faster than vedolizumab.

whyWrong.A: Anti-TNF alone may not be sufficient for severe acute flare.
whyWrong.B: Already failed azathioprine, increasing dose unlikely to help.
whyWrong.C: Vedolizumab has slower onset (6-12 weeks), not ideal for severe flare.
whyWrong.D: Surgery is last resort after failing medical therapy (3 biologics).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Severe UC flare: Steroids (bridge) + Anti-TNF (infliximab). Vedolizumab for maintenance. Surgery if refractory.

vignette: A 45-year-old male presents with jaundice, fatigue, and RUQ pain for 1 week. He ate at a seafood restaurant 4 weeks ago. Labs: AST 1200, ALT 1500, ALP 200, Bilirubin 8.0. Hepatitis serologies: HAV IgM positive. What is the expected outcome?

options.text: Progression to chronic hepatitis in 80% of cases
Acute hepatitis with complete recovery in >99% of cases

explanation.whyCorrect: Acute Hepatitis A: Fecal-oral transmission (contaminated seafood). Self-limited, complete recovery in >99%. NO chronic carrier state. Treatment: Supportive. Prevention: HAV vaccine.

whyWrong.A: HAV does NOT cause chronic hepatitis (unlike HBV and HCV).
whyWrong.C: Fulminant hepatitis is rare (<1%), mostly in elderly or underlying liver disease.
whyWrong.D: HCC risk is with chronic HBV/HCV, not acute HAV.
whyWrong.E: HAV does not progress to cirrhosis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Hepatitis A: Acute only, NEVER chronic. Fecal-oral spread. Complete recovery in >99%. Vaccine available.

vignette: A 30-year-old female presents with chronic diarrhea, weight loss, and megaloblastic anemia. She has a history of recurrent oral ulcers. Small bowel biopsy shows villous atrophy with increased intraepithelial lymphocytes. Which HLA type is MOST strongly associated?

explanation.whyCorrect: Celiac disease: Villous atrophy + intraepithelial lymphocytosis. HLA-DQ2 (90-95%) or HLA-DQ8 (5-10%) is present in >99% of patients. High negative predictive value (if negative, rules out celiac). Associated with dermatitis herpetiformis.

whyWrong.A: HLA-B27: Ankylosing spondylitis, reactive arthritis, IBD-associated arthritis.
whyWrong.D: HLA-DR4: Rheumatoid arthritis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Celiac disease: HLA-DQ2/DQ8. Villous atrophy + IEL. Treatment: Strict gluten-free diet. Check tTG-IgA for screening.

vignette: A 70-year-old male presents with painless jaundice, weight loss, and a palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier sign). CT shows a 3cm mass in the head of the pancreas with biliary dilation. CA 19-9 is elevated. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: Pancreatic adenocarcinoma: Painless jaundice + Courvoisier sign (palpable, non-tender gallbladder) + Weight loss + Mass in head of pancreas. Most common pancreatic malignancy. CA 19-9 is tumor marker (not diagnostic). Treatment: Whipple procedure if resectable.

whyWrong.A: Pseudocyst has history of pancreatitis, not painless jaundice.
whyWrong.B: Chronic pancreatitis causes pain, calcifications, not mass with obstruction.
whyWrong.D: PNETs are less common, often functional (insulin, gastrin), and may not cause jaundice.
whyWrong.E: AIP responds to steroids, has IgG4 elevation, and diffuse 'sausage-shaped' pancreas.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Painless jaundice + Courvoisier sign + Pancreatic head mass = Adenocarcinoma until proven otherwise. Only 15-20% resectable at diagnosis.

vignette: A 50-year-old obese diabetic male has elevated AST 70, ALT 90, and echogenic liver on ultrasound. Hepatitis panel is negative. He drinks 1 glass of wine weekly. What is the diagnosis per AASLD 2024 nomenclature?

explanation.whyCorrect: AASLD/EASL 2024: NAFLD renamed to MASLD (Metabolic dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease). Criteria: Hepatic steatosis + ≥1 cardiometabolic risk factor (obesity, diabetes, hypertension, hypertriglyceridemia, low HDL). Term 'fatty' replaced with 'steatotic' to reduce stigma. MASH (Metabolic dysfunction-Associated Steatohepatitis) replaces NASH. Patient has steatosis + obesity + diabetes = MASLD.

whyWrong.A: NAFLD is outdated terminology (replaced by MASLD in 2024).
whyWrong.B: MASLD: New nomenclature emphasizing metabolic dysfunction, not 'non-alcoholic'.
whyWrong.C: Alcohol intake <20g/day (women) or <30g/day (men) rules out alcoholic liver disease.
whyWrong.D: Autoimmune hepatitis: ANA/ASMA positive, IgG elevated, interface hepatitis on biopsy.
whyWrong.E: Hemochromatosis: Elevated ferritin + Transferrin saturation >45%, HFE gene.

explanation.clinicalPearl: NAFLD is now MASLD (2024). NASH is now MASH. Focus on metabolic dysfunction, not alcohol exclusion. Weight loss 7-10% = cornerstone.

vignette: A 45-year-old male with H. pylori-positive duodenal ulcer has failed clarithromycin-based triple therapy. What is the BEST salvage regimen per ACG 2024?

options.text: Bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI + Bismuth + Tetracycline + Metronidazole) x14 days

explanation.whyCorrect: Failed clarithromycin triple therapy (high clarithromycin resistance in US >15%): Bismuth quadruple therapy (BQT) is recommended salvage. PPI + Bismuth + Tetracycline + Metronidazole x14 days. Achieves >90% eradication. Levofloxacin triple therapy is alternative but resistance increasing. Rifabutin triple therapy is another option. Always confirm eradication (urea breath test or stool antigen) 4 weeks after treatment.

whyWrong.A: Repeating clarithromycin after failure is inappropriate (high resistance).
whyWrong.B: Bismuth quadruple therapy is preferred salvage regimen.
whyWrong.C: Levofloxacin triple therapy is alternative but fluoroquinolone resistance increasing.
whyWrong.D: PPI alone does not eradicate H. pylori.
whyWrong.E: Monotherapy is ineffective and promotes resistance.

explanation.clinicalPearl: H. pylori salvage: Bismuth quadruple therapy (BQT) x14 days. Levofloxacin or Rifabutin triple therapy if BQT fails. Confirm eradication!

vignette: A 50-year-old male with acute pancreatitis (Ranson score 3, BUN 28, Hct 48%) presents within 6 hours of pain onset. What is the BEST fluid resuscitation strategy per AGA 2023?

options.text: Lactated Ringer's solution at 3 mL/kg/hr (aggressive) for first 12-24 hours

explanation.whyCorrect: AGA 2023: Moderate-severe pancreatitis (elevated BUN, hemoconcentration): AGGRESSIVE Lactated Ringer's (LR) at 3 mL/kg/hr for first 12-24 hours, then reassess. LR is PREFERRED over NS (less hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis, anti-inflammatory properties). Goal: Decrease BUN and Hct within 12-24 hours (indicates adequate resuscitation). Avoid over-resuscitation after 24-48h.

whyWrong.A: 1.5 mL/kg/hr is maintenance; this patient needs aggressive resuscitation (3 mL/kg/hr).
whyWrong.B: LR 3 mL/kg/hr = Aggressive resuscitation. LR > NS for pancreatitis.
whyWrong.C: Bolus alone insufficient; needs ongoing aggressive rate.
whyWrong.D: Colloids have no proven benefit over crystalloids in pancreatitis.
whyWrong.E: Fluid restriction contraindicated in acute pancreatitis (third-spacing requires aggressive replacement).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Pancreatitis: AGGRESSIVE LR (3 mL/kg/hr x12-24h). LR > NS. Goal: ↓BUN, ↓Hct. Avoid over-resuscitation after 24-48h.

vignette: A 70-year-old female develops C. difficile diarrhea (PCR positive, non-severe per IDSA criteria) after a course of ciprofloxacin for UTI. What is the FIRST-LINE treatment per IDSA 2024?

options.text: Oral vancomycin 125mg PO QID x10 days OR Fidaxomicin 200mg PO BID x10 days

explanation.whyCorrect: IDSA/SHEA 2021 (still current 2024): Oral vancomycin 125mg QID OR Fidaxomicin 200mg BID are FIRST-LINE for initial non-severe C. diff. Metronidazole is NO LONGER first-line (lower efficacy). Fidaxomicin has lower recurrence rate but higher cost. IV vancomycin has NO effect on C. diff (not excreted into colon). FMT for ≥3 recurrences.

whyWrong.A: Metronidazole is second-line (only if vancomycin/fidaxomicin unavailable).
whyWrong.B: Oral vancomycin or Fidaxomicin are first-line. Fidaxomicin preferred if high recurrence risk.
whyWrong.C: IV vancomycin does NOT reach colonic lumen.
whyWrong.D: FMT is for ≥3 recurrences, not initial episode.
whyWrong.E: Probiotics have limited evidence; not recommended as sole therapy.

explanation.clinicalPearl: C. diff (initial, non-severe): Oral vancomycin 125mg QID OR Fidaxomicin 200mg BID. Metronidazole NO LONGER first-line.

vignette: A 55-year-old male with cirrhosis due to MASLD (Child-Pugh A, MELD 10) asks about HCC screening. What is the recommended method per AASLD 2024?

explanation.whyCorrect: AASLD 2024: HCC surveillance in cirrhosis = Ultrasound + AFP every 6 months. US alone has 45-60% sensitivity; adding AFP increases sensitivity to 63-70%. MRI/CT reserved for: Inadequate US (obesity, steatosis), nodules on US, or rising AFP. CT has radiation risk (not ideal for long-term surveillance). Surveillance improves early detection and survival.

whyWrong.A: CT is not first-line for surveillance (radiation, cost).
whyWrong.B: US + AFP q6mo is the standard surveillance strategy.
whyWrong.C: MRI is reserved for inadequate US or high-risk patients (cost, access).
whyWrong.D: US q12mo has lower sensitivity than q6mo.
whyWrong.E: PET-CT not recommended for HCC surveillance.

explanation.clinicalPearl: HCC surveillance in cirrhosis: US + AFP every 6 months. CT/MRI if US inadequate or abnormal findings.

vignette: A 25-year-old male with dysphagia to solids and history of food impaction has esophageal biopsies showing >15 eosinophils/HPF despite PPI trial. What is the BEST treatment per AGA 2024?

explanation.whyCorrect: EoE refractory to PPI: Dupilumab is the FIRST and ONLY FDA-approved medication for EoE (2022). Anti-IL4/IL13 pathway. Improves symptoms and reduces eosinophilic infiltration. Alternative: Topical swallowed steroids (budesonide slurry or fluticasone) or dietary elimination (6-food elimination diet). Dilation for strictures. Systemic steroids avoided due to side effects.

whyWrong.B: Dupilumab: FDA-approved for EoE. First biologic for EoE.
whyWrong.C: Systemic steroids effective but side effects limit long-term use.
whyWrong.D: Dilation treats strictures, not underlying inflammation.
whyWrong.E: Dietary elimination is effective but challenging to maintain.

explanation.clinicalPearl: EoE refractory to PPI: Dupilumab (FDA-approved 2022). Topical steroids alternative. Dietary elimination is non-pharmacologic option.

vignette: A 60-year-old male with alcoholic cirrhosis (MELD 22, ascites) develops AKI (Cr 2.5 from baseline 1.0) after large-volume paracentesis without albumin. He is not on diuretics. No proteinuria/hematuria. What is the diagnosis per ICA 2024?

explanation.whyCorrect: HRS-AKI (new nomenclature replacing HRS-1): Cirrhosis + Ascites + AKI (Cr increase ≥0.3 within 48h or ≥1.5x baseline) + No response to diuretic withdrawal and albumin challenge (1g/kg x2 days) + No shock, nephrotoxins, proteinuria, or obstruction. Paracentesis without albumin → volume depletion → precipitates HRS. Treatment: Terlipressin + Albumin (FDA-approved 2022) + Liver transplant evaluation.

whyWrong.A: Pre-renal AKI responds to volume expansion; HRS-AKI does not improve after albumin.
whyWrong.B: HRS-AKI: Functional renal failure in cirrhosis. Terlipressin + Albumin = Treatment.
whyWrong.C: ATN: Muddy brown casts, FeNa >2%, history of prolonged hypotension or nephrotoxins.
whyWrong.D: IgA nephropathy: Hematuria, proteinuria, not isolated AKI in cirrhosis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: HRS-AKI: Cirrhosis + Ascites + AKI + No response to albumin. Terlipressin + Albumin (FDA 2022). Liver transplant = definitive.

vignette: A 35-year-old female with moderate-severe UC (Mayo score 8) has failed mesalamine and azathioprine. What is the BEST first-line advanced therapy per AGA 2024?

explanation.whyCorrect: AGA 2024: Upadacitinib (selective JAK1 inhibitor) is now PREFERRED first-line advanced therapy for moderate-severe UC. U-ACHIEVE and U-ACCOMPLISH trials showed superior efficacy vs placebo with rapid onset. Oral administration is advantage over injectables. Higher response rates than vedolizumab and adalimumab in head-to-head trials. Tofacitinib is older JAK inhibitor (more side effects).

whyWrong.A: Anti-TNF is effective but upadacitinib has higher efficacy in recent trials.
whyWrong.B: Upadacitinib: Oral, rapid onset, superior efficacy. Preferred first-line per AGA 2024.
whyWrong.C: Vedolizumab is gut-selective but slower onset and lower efficacy than upadacitinib.
whyWrong.D: Ustekinumab is effective but slower onset than upadacitinib.
whyWrong.E: Tofacitinib is older JAK inhibitor (pan-JAK); upadacitinib is more selective (JAK1).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Moderate-severe UC: Upadacitinib (JAK1 inhibitor) = New first-line advanced therapy. Oral, rapid onset. Superior to vedolizumab.

vignette: A 55-year-old male with long-segment Barrett esophagus (C5M7) has biopsies showing non-dysplastic intestinal metaplasia on 2 consecutive endoscopies. What is the appropriate surveillance interval per ACG 2024?

explanation.whyCorrect: Non-dysplastic Barrett esophagus: Surveillance EGD every 3-5 years. Long-segment (≥3cm) = every 3 years. Short-segment (<3cm) = every 5 years. If low-grade dysplasia: EGD every 6-12 months or consider endoscopic eradication. If high-grade dysplasia: Endoscopic eradication therapy (RFA or EMR). PPI BID to control reflux.

whyWrong.A: Every 3 months is too frequent for non-dysplastic BE.
whyWrong.B: Every 3-5 years is standard for non-dysplastic BE based on segment length.
whyWrong.C: Annual surveillance is for low-grade dysplasia, not non-dysplastic.
whyWrong.D: Endoscopic eradication is for high-grade dysplasia or intramucosal carcinoma.
whyWrong.E: Non-dysplastic BE requires ongoing surveillance (risk of progression 0.3%/year).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Barrett surveillance: Non-dysplastic → q3-5yr. Low-grade dysplasia → q6-12mo or eradication. High-grade dysplasia → Eradication therapy.

vignette: A 45-year-old male with chronic alcoholic pancreatitis has debilitating epigastric pain radiating to the back despite abstinence and pancreatic enzymes. CT shows calcifications and dilated pancreatic duct (7mm). What is the BEST next step?

options.text: ERCP with pancreatic duct stenting or ESWL (lithotripsy) for stones

explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic pancreatitis with dilated duct >5mm and stones: Endoscopic therapy (ERCP with stenting ± ESWL for pancreatic duct stones) is first-line intervention. Provides pain relief by decompressing obstructed duct. If endoscopic therapy fails: Surgical drainage (Puestow/lateral pancreaticojejunostomy). Celiac plexus block provides temporary relief only. Total pancreatectomy is last resort.

whyWrong.A: Escalating opioids risks dependence and opioid-induced hyperalgesia.
whyWrong.B: ERCP + ESWL targets the mechanical cause of pain (ductal obstruction).
whyWrong.C: Total pancreatectomy is last resort (brittle diabetes).
whyWrong.D: Celiac block is temporary (weeks-months) and does not address obstruction.
whyWrong.E: Medical management has failed; mechanical obstruction needs intervention.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Chronic pancreatitis pain + Dilated duct + Stones: ERCP + ESWL first. Surgery if endoscopic therapy fails. Opioids last resort.
